findings.title: Potential ReDoS vulnerability
findings.description:
Regex with nested quantifiers may cause catastrophic backtracking, leading to denial of service.

**Analysis:**
🔍 Pattern issue: nested quantifiers detected
findings.why_it_matters:
ReDoS attacks exploit regex backtracking to consume CPU:
• A malicious input can take exponential time to evaluate
• Single requests can freeze the server
• Input like 'aaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaaa!' on /(a+)+$/ can hang

findings.title: High panic density in `new`: 4 panic-family calls
findings.description: [COMPOUND: 2 co-located issues] Function `new` contains 4 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 93, line 96, line 102, line 106. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.

findings.title: Circular dependency: 4 files
findings.description:
Mutual dependency between: snippets.rs ↔ active.rs ↔ elaborate.rs ↔ render.rs

**Weakest link to break:** `snippets.rs` → `active.rs` (1 import)
findings.suggested_fix:
🔗 **Best place to break:** `snippets.rs` → `active.rs` (weakest coupling: 1 imports)

Since `snippets.rs` only imports 1 symbol(s) from `active.rs`, consider:
- Move those symbols to a shared module
- Pass them as parameters instead of importing
- Use dependency injection

**For small cycles:**
1. Merge tightly coupled modules if they're always used together
2. Use TYPE_CHECKING (Python) or type-only imports (TS) for type hints
3. Extract common code to a third module
findings.why_it_matters: Circular dependencies make code harder to understand, test, and maintain. They can cause import errors at runtime and make it difficult to refactor individual modules.

findings.title: High panic density in `print`: 5 panic-family calls
findings.description: Function `print` contains 5 calls to `.unwrap()`, `.expect()`, or `panic!()` at line 62, line 88, line 119, line 121, line 127. This makes the function fragile and likely to panic at runtime.
findings.suggested_fix:
Reduce panic points by:
- Using `?` operator for error propagation
- Using `unwrap_or` / `unwrap_or_default` / `unwrap_or_else`
- Returning `Result<T, E>` instead of panicking
- Extracting fallible logic into a helper that returns Result
findings.why_it_matters: Functions with many panic points are fragile. A single unexpected None/Err will crash the program. Consolidating error handling makes code more robust and easier to reason about.
